categories.desc: RAISE/BUILD discretionary infrastructure grants, Essential Air Service, Build America Bureau credit programs, and DOT departmental management
categories.details: The Office of the Secretary (OST) administers several cross-modal programs. The RAISE (formerly BUILD, formerly TIGER) grant program ($345M in FY2025) awards competitive discretionary grants to state, local, and tribal governments for transportation infrastructure projects that have national or regional significance. The Essential Air Service program ($450M) subsidizes commercial air service to small and rural communities that otherwise would not be served. The Build America Bureau provides federal loan guarantees and credit assistance for large infrastructure projects under TIFIA and RRIF. Also included: OIG ($121M) and GLSDC ($40M).

categories.title: Natural Resources & Environment
categories.desc: Public lands, environmental protection, water projects, and natural resource stewardship

categories.title: Conservation & Land Management
categories.desc: Stewardship of national forests, wildlife refuges, and public lands (OMB subfunction 302)

categories.title: Forest Service
categories.desc: National forest management, wildfire suppression, and forest health
categories.details: FY2025 appropriations of $8.6B in regular discretionary funding plus $945M in IIJA supplemental funding. Manages 193 million acres of national forests and grasslands. The largest share goes to wildfire management — paying for firefighter crews, helicopters, and firebreak construction. Also funds forest restoration after fire, invasive species removal, and recreational trail maintenance. In practical terms: if your town borders a national forest, this is what pays to thin overgrown trees to reduce fire risk, build the trail you hike on, and send wildfire crews when flames get close.

categories.title: Fish & Wildlife Service
categories.desc: Wildlife refuges, endangered species recovery, and habitat restoration
categories.details: Manages the 568-unit National Wildlife Refuge System (over 850 million acres). Runs the Endangered Species Act — listing threatened species, writing recovery plans, and designating critical habitat. Also distributes excise-tax-funded grants to states for wildlife and sport fish restoration (Pittman-Robertson and Dingell-Johnson Acts). In practical terms: this pays for the biologists surveying bald eagle nests, the hatchery restocking salmon in your local river, and the refuge where migratory birds stop each spring.

categories.title: Bureau of Land Management
categories.desc: Public lands stewardship, grazing, mining oversight, and recreation
categories.details: Manages roughly 245 million surface acres — more than any other federal agency — mostly in the western U.S. and Alaska. Handles grazing permits for ranchers, energy and mineral leasing, wildfire management on BLM land, and recreation areas. In practical terms: if you camp on BLM land, ride an off-road vehicle on designated trails, or a rancher grazes cattle on public rangeland near your community, this is the agency and money that manages it.

categories.title: Farm Bill Conservation Programs
categories.desc: USDA conservation programs for private farmland and ranchland
categories.details: Mandatory spending authorized through the Farm Bill. Includes programs like the Conservation Reserve Program (pays farmers to take fragile cropland out of production), the Environmental Quality Incentives Program (cost-shares for farmers to install conservation practices like cover crops or stream buffers), and the Conservation Stewardship Program. In practical terms: if a farmer in your county plants cover crops to stop topsoil from washing into the local creek, or retires highly erodible hillside land to restore grassland, these programs are probably paying part of the cost.

This subfunction covers the care and management of federal lands and natural habitats, plus conservation programs for private farmland. A large and growing share comes from mandatory Farm Bill conservation programs administered by USDA. The rest funds the agencies that manage forests, wildlife refuges, and public lands. The FY2025 total is estimated by applying the OMB Table 3.2 subfunction 302 share (31.8%) to the verified $88.3B total.

categories.title: Pollution Control & Abatement
categories.desc: EPA programs that enforce clean air/water standards, clean up toxic sites, and fund local water infrastructure (OMB subfunction 304)

categories.title: EPA Environmental Programs & Management
categories.desc: Core EPA regulatory programs, enforcement, permitting, and research
categories.details: FY2025 regular appropriation of ~$3.19B. This is EPA's operating budget — the money that pays the scientists who review new chemicals for safety, the inspectors who check whether factories and power plants are following pollution limits, and the staff who write and enforce rules under the Clean Air Act and Clean Water Act. In practical terms: this is why the air you breathe and the water from your tap meet safety standards. It also pays for climate research, environmental justice grants, and PFAS contamination investigations.

categories.title: Superfund & Hazardous Waste Cleanup
categories.desc: Cleaning up the nation's worst toxic contamination sites
categories.details: Funded through regular appropriations (~$537M), the reinstated Superfund excise tax, and IIJA supplemental funds (~$3.5B over multiple years). Pays for investigation and cleanup at roughly 1,300+ Superfund National Priorities List sites — former factories, landfills, and military bases with hazardous contamination. In practical terms: if a neighborhood near you has contaminated groundwater from a shuttered industrial site, Superfund is the program that tests the soil, provides bottled water during the cleanup, removes contaminated dirt, and monitors the site for decades.

categories.title: Water Infrastructure Grants (SRFs & IIJA)
categories.desc: Federal grants to states for clean water and safe drinking water systems
categories.details: FY2025 total includes $3.04B in regular appropriations for State Revolving Funds (SRFs) plus $11.62B in IIJA advance appropriations through EPA's STAG account. States use SRF grants to make low-interest loans to local water utilities that need to upgrade treatment plants, replace lead pipes, or build stormwater systems. In practical terms: when your city replaces old lead water pipes, upgrades the sewage treatment plant to stop overflow into the river, or installs new filtration to meet drinking water standards, the money often comes from these programs.

categories.title: State & Tribal Environmental Grants
categories.desc: Grants to states and tribes for air quality monitoring, water pollution control, and other environmental programs
categories.details: Includes categorical grants within the STAG account for state and tribal air quality programs (~$236M), water pollution control (Section 106), underground injection control, pesticide programs, and more. States use these to run their own environmental agencies — the front-line regulators that issue permits, inspect facilities, and monitor pollution in your community. In practical terms: the air quality monitor that tells you whether today's air is safe to breathe, or the state inspector who checks that your local factory isn't dumping into the river — those are largely paid for by these grants.

This subfunction covers EPA's regulatory and cleanup programs plus federal grants that help states and localities protect air, water, and land from pollution. FY2025 saw a major jump in outlays — roughly triple FY2024 — driven largely by IIJA infrastructure money and Inflation Reduction Act funds flowing through EPA to states and communities. The FY2025 total is estimated by applying the OMB Table 3.2 subfunction 304 share (27.5%) to the verified $88.3B total.

categories.title: Water Resources
categories.desc: Dams, waterways, flood control, and federal water infrastructure (OMB subfunction 301)

categories.title: Army Corps of Engineers Civil Works
categories.desc: Flood control, navigation, ecosystem restoration, and hydropower
categories.details: FY2025 appropriation of $8.703B. The Army Corps manages roughly 700+ dams, 25,000+ miles of navigable channels, and hundreds of flood-control projects. The operations & maintenance account (~64% of total) funds day-to-day upkeep of locks, dams, and harbors that keep commercial shipping moving on the Mississippi, Ohio, and Columbia rivers. Construction funds build or upgrade flood walls, levees, and ecosystem restoration projects. In practical terms: the levee protecting your river town from flooding, the lock that lets barges carry grain to port, and the beach replenishment project protecting your coastline — those are Corps projects.

categories.title: Bureau of Reclamation
categories.desc: Western water supply, irrigation, dams, and hydropower
categories.details: FY2025 appropriation (est. ~$1.55B regular plus IIJA supplements). Reclamation manages water infrastructure across 17 western states — including Hoover Dam, Grand Coulee Dam, and hundreds of smaller projects. Supplies irrigation water to roughly 10 million acres of farmland and municipal/industrial water to 31 million people. In practical terms: if you live in the western U.S. and turn on your faucet, there's a good chance Reclamation built or manages the dam, canal, or pipeline that delivers your water.

categories.title: Other Water Resource Programs
categories.desc: Tennessee Valley Authority, other federal water and flood programs, and Farm Bill water-related conservation
categories.details: Includes other federal water resource spending that falls under subfunction 301 — Tennessee Valley Authority power and stewardship programs, smaller water project agencies, and the water-related portions of Farm Bill mandatory conservation spending (e.g., watershed programs). In practical terms: this covers the other pieces of water infrastructure the federal government funds beyond the Army Corps and Reclamation.

This subfunction covers the engineering and construction side of water — the dams, levees, locks, canals, and flood-control systems the federal government builds and maintains. The Army Corps of Engineers is the dominant agency. Bureau of Reclamation handles western irrigation and water supply. The FY2025 total is estimated by applying the OMB Table 3.2 subfunction 301 share (18.5%) to the verified $88.3B total.

categories.title: Recreational Resources
categories.desc: National parks visitor services, historic preservation, and outdoor recreation (OMB subfunction 303)

categories.title: National Park Service
categories.desc: Operating 431 national park units, maintaining visitor facilities, and preserving cultural sites
categories.details: FY2025 discretionary appropriation of $3.337B plus estimated $1.24B in mandatory funding (recreation fees, concession fees, Great American Outdoors Act land acquisition). The $2.894B Operation of the National Park System account pays for ranger-led programs, trail upkeep, search and rescue, and facility maintenance at all 431 units. Construction ($172M) addresses the agency's $23B deferred maintenance backlog. NPS also received ~$1.3B from the Legacy Restoration Fund for deferred maintenance (final year of GAOA authorization). In practical terms: the ranger who leads your kids on a nature walk, the maintained road into Yellowstone, the restored historic building at Gettysburg — NPS pays for all of it.

categories.title: Historic Preservation Fund & Heritage Programs
categories.desc: Grants for state and tribal historic preservation, heritage areas, and cultural sites
categories.details: Includes the NPS Historic Preservation Fund (~$169M), National Recreation and Preservation account (~$90M), and other heritage programs. The HPF funds grants to State Historic Preservation Offices and Tribal Historic Preservation Offices, competitive grants for historically Black colleges, Save America's Treasures grants, and civil rights history sites. In practical terms: when your town restores a historic downtown building using federal matching grants, or a tribal nation preserves an ancestral cultural site, these programs often provide the funding.

categories.title: Land & Water Conservation Fund
categories.desc: Land acquisition and outdoor recreation grants funded by offshore energy revenues
categories.details: The LWCF receives $900M annually in mandatory funding (made permanent by the Great American Outdoors Act). Money is split between federal land acquisition (buying inholdings within park and refuge boundaries) and matching grants to states for local outdoor recreation — playgrounds, ball fields, boat ramps, and trails. In practical terms: if your community built a new public park or renovated a playground with a 'Land and Water Conservation Fund' sign on it, that's this program.

categories.title: Other Recreation Programs
categories.desc: Smithsonian outdoor programs, Indian arts and culture, and other recreation-related spending
categories.details: Includes other spending classified under subfunction 303 — recreation-related programs at other agencies, Gateway and Urban Park programs, Centennial Challenge matching funds, and related recreation and preservation activities. In practical terms: these are the smaller programs that support outdoor recreation, cultural preservation, and visitor services across various federal agencies.

This subfunction covers the visitor-facing side of public lands — the parks you visit, the historic sites you tour, and the recreation grants that build local trails and playgrounds. The National Park Service dominates, but the Land and Water Conservation Fund and Historic Preservation Fund are significant standalone programs. The FY2025 total is estimated by applying the OMB Table 3.2 subfunction 303 share (8.7%) to the verified $88.3B total.

categories.title: Other Natural Resources
categories.desc: Ocean services, earth science, weather forecasting, and other resource programs (OMB subfunction 306)

categories.title: NOAA Oceans, Coasts & Weather
categories.desc: Weather forecasting, ocean science, fisheries management, and coastal services
categories.details: NOAA's budget covers the National Weather Service (forecasts, hurricane warnings, tornado alerts), the National Ocean Service (coastal mapping, marine sanctuaries), fisheries science and management, satellite operations, and ocean research. In practical terms: the weather forecast on your phone, the hurricane evacuation warning that saves lives, the fishing regulations that keep commercial and recreational fisheries sustainable, and the tide charts mariners rely on — NOAA provides all of these.

categories.title: USGS Earth Science
categories.desc: Earthquake monitoring, mapping, water resources research, and natural hazards science
categories.details: USGS operates the earthquake early warning system (ShakeAlert), the national network of stream gauges that monitor river levels and flood risk, the Landsat satellite program for earth observation, and the National Map. Also researches volcanic hazards, landslides, and mineral resources. In practical terms: the earthquake alert on your phone, the stream gauge data that tells your county whether the river is about to flood, and the topographic maps used by hikers and first responders — USGS provides these.

categories.title: Mining Reclamation & Mineral Programs
categories.desc: Abandoned mine cleanup, mineral leasing, and mine safety regulation
categories.details: Includes the Office of Surface Mining Reclamation and Enforcement (which cleans up abandoned coal mines and distributes fees to states for mine reclamation), mineral leasing administration, and related programs. In practical terms: if an abandoned coal mine near your town is leaking acid drainage into a stream, this program pays to plug the mine, treat the water, and restore the landscape.

categories.title: Other Resource & Ocean Programs
categories.desc: Marine protection, coastal zone management, and other natural resource activities
categories.details: Includes remaining subfunction 306 spending: coastal zone management grants, marine mammal protection, deep-water research, international fisheries agreements, and other natural resource programs spread across multiple agencies. In practical terms: these are the smaller but still important programs that protect coastlines from erosion, fund marine biology research, manage migratory fish treaties with other countries, and support ocean exploration.

This subfunction covers the science and service agencies that monitor the earth, oceans, and atmosphere, plus mining reclamation and other resource programs. NOAA is the largest component. The FY2025 total is estimated by applying the OMB Table 3.2 subfunction 306 share (13.6%) to the verified $88.3B total.

Budget Function 300 — Natural Resources and Environment — covers public lands, environmental protection, water infrastructure, and natural resource science. FY2025 actual outlays were $88.3 billion, a 55% increase over FY2024 ($56.9B), driven primarily by Inflation Reduction Act and Bipartisan Infrastructure Law money flowing through EPA and conservation programs. The top-line total ($88,312M) is confirmed from the FY2025 Combined Statement of Receipts, Outlays, and Balances. Subfunction splits are estimated by applying OMB Historical Table 3.2 (FY2025 President's Budget) proportional shares to the actual total, since final subfunction actuals were not yet available at time of writing. Sub-agency figures use FY2025 enacted appropriations from CRS reports (appropriations ≠ outlays, but they are the best primary-source data available at the sub-agency level).

source_notes.subfunction_method: OMB Historical Table 3.2 FY2025 estimates proportionally scaled to $88.312B actual (via GovInfo BUDGET-2025-TAB)
source_notes.agency_data: FY2025 enacted appropriations from Congressional Research Service reports (congress.gov/crs-product/). Note: appropriations (budget authority) differ from outlays. Agency outlays may differ from appropriations shown.
source_notes.limitations: Sub-agency figures are FY2025 appropriations, not final outlays. Subfunction totals are proportional estimates. Final FY2025 subfunction actuals will be published in the FY2027 President's Budget Historical Tables (expected early 2026).

categories.title: Administration of Justice
categories.desc: Federal policing, courts, prisons, and other justice-system operations

categories.title: Federal Law Enforcement
categories.desc: Major federal investigative and enforcement agencies plus related operations

categories.title: FBI
categories.desc: Federal Bureau of Investigation operations
categories.details: The FBI is the federal government's primary law enforcement and domestic intelligence agency, with over 35,000 employees. This pays for counterterrorism, cybercrime, organized crime, and civil rights investigations across all 50 states. FY2025 appropriations were roughly $10.7 billion under the Full-Year Continuing Resolution (P.L. 119-4). About 60% of the FBI budget is classified as defense spending tied to national security and counterterrorism work.

categories.title: DEA
categories.desc: Drug Enforcement Administration operations
categories.details: The DEA enforces federal drug laws and goes after trafficking networks — cartels, fentanyl distributors, and international drug organizations. About 10,000 agents, analysts, and staff work on domestic and international cases. The FY2025 budget request was ~$3.3 billion, slightly up from the FY2024 level. The agency has one of the largest overseas footprints of any U.S. law enforcement agency.

categories.title: ATF
categories.desc: Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives operations
categories.details: ATF enforces federal firearms and explosives laws and investigates arson and illegal trafficking of guns. It runs the National Tracing Center, which helps law enforcement nationwide trace crime guns. FY2025 funding was approximately $1.63 billion under the Continuing Resolution at roughly the FY2024 enacted level. Congress has debated increasing ATF's budget significantly, with some members requesting an increase to $2 billion.

categories.title: U.S. Marshals
categories.desc: Judicial security, fugitive operations, and prisoner transport
categories.details: The U.S. Marshals Service is the nation's oldest federal law enforcement agency. It protects federal judges and courthouses, hunts fugitives, transports federal prisoners between facilities, and manages the federal witness protection program. FY2025 funding was approximately $3.9 billion, which also covers the Federal Prisoner Detention account (a separate account that was increased to $2.236 billion under the CR).

categories.title: Other Federal Enforcement
categories.desc: Interagency law enforcement, COPS grants, and other enforcement activities
categories.details: This covers several smaller enforcement-related accounts within DOJ including the Interagency Crime and Drug Enforcement program, the Community Oriented Policing Services (COPS) grant program, and other law enforcement support functions. COPS grants help local police departments hire officers and implement crime-reduction strategies. These programs collectively support state, local, and tribal law enforcement partnerships.

This covers the major federal agencies that investigate crimes, enforce federal laws, and patrol the nation's borders. The FBI, DEA, ATF, and Marshals each have distinct missions — counterterrorism, drug enforcement, firearms crimes, and fugitive apprehension. Together they make up the front line of federal law enforcement.

categories.title: Other Justice
categories.desc: DOJ legal work, immigration courts, grants, and other justice support

categories.title: Legal Activities
categories.desc: U.S. Attorneys, general legal activities, and national security divisions
categories.details: The 94 U.S. Attorney offices across the country are the federal government's frontline prosecutors. They handle criminal cases ranging from fraud and drug trafficking to terrorism and civil rights violations, and represent the United States in civil cases. FY2025 funding was approximately $2.6 billion. This also includes the General Legal Activities account (which covers main DOJ divisions) and the National Security Division.

categories.title: Civil & Regulatory Divisions
categories.desc: Civil, antitrust, civil rights, environment, and tax litigation divisions
categories.details: These DOJ divisions handle civil litigation where the federal government is a party — defending agencies from lawsuits, pursuing antitrust enforcement, protecting civil rights, prosecuting environmental violations, and handling tax fraud cases. They don't make arrests but win or defend hundreds of billions of dollars in cases annually. Funding reflects primarily attorney salaries and litigation costs.

categories.title: Immigration Review
categories.desc: Executive Office for Immigration Review (immigration courts)
categories.details: The Executive Office for Immigration Review (EOIR) runs the nation's immigration court system — the judges and tribunals that decide whether a non-citizen can remain in the United States. With a backlog of over 3 million pending cases, this office is under intense strain. FY2025 funding was approximately $833 million, though demand far exceeds current capacity.

categories.title: State & Local Assistance
categories.desc: Grants to state, local, and tribal law enforcement and victim services
categories.details: This covers DOJ grant programs that flow money to states, cities, and tribes for crime reduction, victim services, and justice improvements. It includes the State and Local Law Enforcement Assistance account, the Office on Violence Against Women, and the Juvenile Justice programs. These grants help local police hire officers, support crime victims, fund drug courts, and reduce recidivism.

This bucket covers the legal, prosecutorial, and support side of the Justice Department — the prosecutors, immigration judges, and grant programs that back up law enforcement work. The U.S. Attorneys prosecute federal cases, immigration courts rule on deportation proceedings, and grants help communities at the local level.

categories.title: Federal Courts
categories.desc: Operations of the Supreme Court, lower courts, and defender services

categories.title: Trial & Appellate Courts
categories.desc: District, appellate, and bankruptcy courts — salaries, security, and operations
categories.details: The 94 federal district courts and 12 circuit courts of appeals hear and decide federal criminal and civil cases. The FY2025 discretionary request for salaries and expenses alone was $6.4 billion, covering judges, clerks, court reporters, and security. Fees collected from court filings offset some appropriated costs. Bankruptcy courts and the Court of International Trade are also included here.

categories.title: Defender Services
categories.desc: Federal public defenders and court-appointed private counsel for indigent defendants
categories.details: The Sixth Amendment guarantees legal representation to anyone facing federal criminal charges who can't afford an attorney. This account funds the 81 federal public defender and community defender offices, plus compensation for private panel attorneys appointed by courts. The FY2025 request was $1.7 billion — a 16.5% increase over FY2024 driven by higher caseloads from increased prosecutions.

categories.title: Judicial Agencies
categories.desc: Supreme Court, Administrative Office of Courts, and other judicial support
categories.details: This covers the U.S. Supreme Court ($160 million requested for FY2025), the Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts (which manages federal court operations nationwide), the Federal Judicial Center (judicial education and research), and court security. The Supreme Court's budget covers nine justices, roughly 400 employees, and the upkeep of its building on Capitol Hill.

The federal judiciary is a separate branch of government — independent from both Congress and the executive branch. This covers the entire Article III court system from the Supreme Court down to district courts, plus the public defenders who represent those who can't afford attorneys. The judiciary's FY2025 request totaled approximately $10.25 billion ($9.4B discretionary + $852M mandatory).

categories.title: Federal Prisons
categories.desc: Bureau of Prisons operations, facilities, and inmate programs

categories.title: Operations
categories.desc: Bureau of Prisons salaries and daily operating expenses for ~150,000 inmates
categories.details: The Bureau of Prisons (BOP) operates 122 federal prisons housing approximately 150,000 inmates. The salaries and expenses account pays for correctional officers, healthcare, food, and programs like vocational training and drug treatment (including expanded medication-assisted treatment under the First Step Act). FY2025 funding was approximately $8.4 billion, making BOP one of DOJ's largest single expenditures.

categories.title: Buildings & Facilities
categories.desc: Prison construction, renovation, and facility upgrades
categories.details: Federal prison facilities age and deteriorate, requiring ongoing capital investment. The Buildings and Facilities account pays for major construction, renovation, and critical repairs. FY2025 funding was approximately $180 million — historically lower than need, with a maintenance backlog running into the billions. The FY2026 House bill proposed increasing this account by $100 million (+55.6%) to address the backlog.

categories.title: Other Prison Support
categories.desc: Federal Prisoner Detention (pretrial housing in local jails) and other costs
categories.details: This covers costs outside BOP's direct operations, including the Federal Prisoner Detention account, which reimburses local jails and state facilities for housing federal pretrial detainees when BOP facilities are full or unavailable. The FY2025 Continuing Resolution increased the Federal Prisoner Detention account specifically to $2.236 billion — that amount is captured above under the Marshals (who administer it), with only residual support costs here.

The Bureau of Prisons is the federal government's prison system, housing people convicted of federal crimes — primarily drug offenses, firearms violations, immigration crimes, and white-collar fraud. It's one of the largest employers in the Justice Department. FY2025 total BOP funding was approximately $8.65 billion across all accounts.

This is the part of the budget that pays for federal law enforcement, courts, and prisons. It covers agencies that investigate crimes, prosecute cases, run the court system, and house federal inmates. The FY2025 total of approximately $85.2 billion reflects the full Administration of Justice budget function (Function 750), funded primarily through the Full-Year Continuing Resolution (P.L. 119-4) signed March 15, 2025.

categories.title: Community & Regional Development
categories.desc: Federal spending to help communities prepare for and recover from disasters, invest in housing and local development, and strengthen historically underserved regions.
categories.details: Federal Community & Regional Development helps communities prepare for disasters, recover from them, and invest in local economic development. It funds FEMA's disaster response and preparedness work, provides flexible grants that cities and states use for housing and neighborhood improvements, and targets federal investment at economically distressed regions like Appalachia and the Mississippi Delta.

notableExpenses.title: Hurricane Helene Recovery
notableExpenses.desc: A major FY2025 FEMA recovery push in North Carolina after Helene's catastrophic flooding and infrastructure damage.
notableExpenses.details: By September 22, 2025, FEMA said that since January 20, 2025 more than $510 million in Public Assistance reimbursements had been approved to support recovery in North Carolina after Hurricane Helene. The reimbursements cover road and bridge repairs, debris removal, water systems, and other public infrastructure, making this a concrete FY2025 disaster-recovery snapshot rather than a lifetime storm-cost estimate.

categories.title: Disaster Relief
categories.desc: FEMA disaster response, recovery, preparedness, and hazard mitigation programs.
categories.details: Disaster relief covers all FEMA programs funded through the Disaster Relief Fund (DRF), preparedness grants to states and localities, and flood/hazard mitigation programs. Spending swings dramatically year to year based on major disaster declarations. This total is an estimate; the MTS does not publish a single line for this sub-function.

categories.title: Disaster Relief Fund (DRF)
categories.desc: FEMA's primary account for responding to Presidentially declared major disasters and emergencies.
categories.details: The DRF is what pays for cleanup, rebuilding, and direct aid after hurricanes, wildfires, floods, and other federally declared disasters. When a disaster is declared, FEMA can send money to homeowners for temporary housing, help cities repair roads and schools, and fund long-term recovery projects.

categories.title: Preparedness Grants
categories.desc: Grants to states, tribes, and localities to build emergency management capacity before disasters strike.
categories.details: These grants fund state and local emergency management offices, first-responder training, and planning for worst-case scenarios. It includes the Emergency Management Performance Grant (EMPG), State Homeland Security Program and Urban Area Security Initiative. In plain terms, this money helps your local emergency manager keep the lights on, run drills, and be ready when a disaster hits.

categories.title: Flood & Hazard Mitigation
categories.desc: Programs to reduce future flood damage, including property buyouts, home elevations, and the National Flood Insurance Program.
categories.details: FEMA's Hazard Mitigation Grant Program (HMGP), Building Resilient Infrastructure and Communities (BRIC), and Flood Mitigation Assistance (FMA) pay for things like buying out flood-prone homes, elevating properties, and strengthening infrastructure before the next storm. FY2025 was an unusual year: FEMA canceled the $600M FMA grant opportunity in February 2025 and issued no new BRIC awards, so actual new grants were near zero — but prior-year commitments continued to disburse. This total reflects estimated ongoing disbursements from prior-year awards.

categories.title: Community Development
categories.desc: HUD block grants and targeted funding to help cities and states invest in housing, economic development, and neighborhood revitalization.
categories.details: This covers the main HUD community development programs that flow money directly to cities, counties, and states as flexible grants. HUD distributed over $30 billion in total grants in FY2025, but the majority of that (e.g., rental assistance, public housing) sits in other budget functions, not Function 450.

categories.title: CDBG
categories.desc: Community Development Block Grants — flexible federal dollars that cities and counties use for local priorities.
categories.details: CDBG gives cities and counties a block of flexible federal money to spend on local priorities — fixing up housing, repairing infrastructure in low-income neighborhoods, or funding job training. At least 70% must benefit low- or moderate-income people. This is the grant side: one-time capital dollars that fund specific projects, not ongoing monthly payments.

categories.title: HOME
categories.desc: HOME Investment Partnerships — formula grants that states and cities use exclusively for affordable housing.
categories.details: HOME grants go exclusively to affordable housing projects — building new units, rehabbing existing ones, or helping low-income people buy a first home. HOME often provides the gap financing that makes a project financially viable. This is the grant side: it funds construction and development, not the monthly rent subsidies that keep tenants housed long-term.

categories.title: EDA Grants
categories.desc: Economic Development Administration grants that fund infrastructure and job-creation projects in distressed communities.
categories.details: EDA funds things like industrial parks, broadband infrastructure, workforce training centers, and port improvements in areas with high unemployment or recent economic shocks.

categories.title: Other Community Development
categories.desc: Other HUD grants including homeless assistance, Native American housing, housing for the elderly, and targeted community programs.
categories.details: This bucket covers several smaller HUD programs within Function 450: Emergency Solutions Grants for homeless services ($290M), Native American Housing Block Grants (~$1.5B), Housing Opportunities for Persons with AIDS (HOPWA, $505M), and other community-targeted programs. These grants go to tribal housing authorities, nonprofits, and local governments to serve specific populations. Note: Section 8 rental assistance and public housing are in a separate budget function and not included here.

categories.title: Regional Development
categories.desc: Place-based federal investment in economically distressed regions, including Appalachia, the Mississippi Delta, and tribal lands.
categories.details: Regional development programs are among the smallest pieces of Function 450. They target specific geographies that have persistently lagged the national economy — rural Appalachian coal communities, the Mississippi Delta, and tribal nations.

categories.title: Appalachian Region
categories.desc: Appalachian Regional Commission (ARC) grants for economic and community development across 13 states.
categories.details: ARC invests in the 423-county Appalachian region stretching from New York to Mississippi — areas hit hard by the decline of coal, manufacturing, and the opioid crisis. Money goes to broadband infrastructure, workforce training, water systems, and small business development, all decided through a partnership between the federal government and 13 state governors.

categories.title: Delta Region
categories.desc: Delta Regional Authority (DRA) grants for the eight-state Mississippi Delta region.
categories.details: The Delta Regional Authority serves the poorest counties in one of the nation's most economically distressed areas — the Mississippi River Delta across eight states, covering about 10 million people. DRA funds workforce development, infrastructure, and business development. Annual appropriations are modest, around $35 million.

categories.title: Tribal Community Programs
categories.desc: Federal grants for tribal economic development and community infrastructure within Function 450.
categories.details: The largest tribal housing program in Function 450 is HUD's Native American Housing Block Grant (NAHBG), funded at approximately $1.2 billion in FY2025, with additional funds for tribal colleges and community development. These dollars go to tribal housing authorities to build and maintain homes on reservations and in tribal communities — places where housing shortages and infrastructure deficits are severe. Note: Many tribal programs sit in other budget functions (Interior, HHS, BIA); this reflects only the HUD/Function-450 portion.

categories.title: Other Regional Programs
categories.desc: Other federal regional commissions and economic development programs, including the Denali Commission (Alaska) and Northern Border Regional Commission.
categories.details: Several other regional commissions serve specific geographies: the Denali Commission funds rural Alaska infrastructure, the Northern Border Regional Commission covers rural New England and upstate New York, the Southeast Crescent Regional Commission covers the rural South, and the Southwest Border Regional Commission serves border communities. Combined annual appropriations for all of these are relatively small — well under $200 million. The remainder of this line item reflects residual Function 450 spending from agencies like the Department of Commerce and SBA that doesn't fit cleanly into other sub-categories.

categories.title: Education & Social Services
categories.desc: Federal education aid, workforce programs, and social services for children and families (Budget Function 500)

categories.title: K-12 Education
categories.desc: Formula and targeted grants that support public schools and special education

categories.title: Title I
categories.desc: Grants to public schools serving high concentrations of low-income students
categories.details: Title I is the largest federal K-12 grant program, sending money to roughly 90% of U.S. school districts. Schools use it to hire extra teachers, provide tutoring, and run after-school programs for students in high-poverty neighborhoods. Funds flow from the federal government to states, then to local districts based on census poverty data. The FY2025 appropriation includes both current-year funds and an advance payment that will be spent in the following school year.

categories.title: IDEA
categories.desc: Special education grants under the Individuals with Disabilities Education Act
categories.details: IDEA requires public schools to provide a free, appropriate education to every child with a disability — from autism and learning disabilities to physical impairments. Most of this money flows as formula grants to states, which pass it to local school districts to pay for special education teachers, therapists, and assistive devices. FY2025 enacted funding includes grants to states, preschool grants, and early intervention grants for infants and toddlers with disabilities.

categories.title: Impact Aid
categories.desc: Support for school districts affected by federal land ownership or military bases
categories.details: When federal land — like a military base, national park, or Native American reservation — sits inside a school district, that land generates no local property tax. Impact Aid compensates those districts for the lost revenue so they can still fund teachers and schools. About 1,200 districts nationwide receive these payments, with the largest going to districts serving military families or communities near federal installations.

categories.title: Other K-12 Grants
categories.desc: School improvement, English language acquisition, and other elementary and secondary grants
categories.details: This covers targeted K-12 programs outside the major formula grants, including funding for English language learners, rural schools, charter school grants, Native American education, school safety, and school improvement initiatives. Each program is smaller on its own but addresses specific student populations that Title I and IDEA do not fully cover.

This part of the budget covers formula and targeted grants that support public schools and special education. Title I and IDEA alone account for more than $33 billion and together reach nearly every school district in America. Enacted amounts reflect the Full-Year Continuing Appropriations Act (P.L. 119-4), which generally held programs at FY2024 levels.

categories.title: Social Services
categories.desc: Early childhood, child care, and family-service programs

categories.title: Head Start
categories.desc: Early childhood education and family services for low-income children birth to age 5
categories.details: Head Start provides preschool, health screenings, meals, and family support services to roughly one million low-income children each year. Local nonprofits and community organizations run Head Start centers using direct federal grants.

categories.title: Child Care
categories.desc: Child Care and Development Fund subsidies helping low-income families pay for child care
categories.details: The Child Care and Development Fund (CCDF) helps low-income working parents afford licensed child care by issuing vouchers or subsidies. States receive block grant funds and let eligible families choose their own providers — including family day cares, centers, and relatives. Without this subsidy, child care costs for many families would exceed their rent.

categories.title: SSBG
categories.desc: Social Services Block Grant giving states flexible funding for social service programs
categories.details: The Social Services Block Grant gives states broad flexibility to fund protective services — including child abuse prevention, foster care support, services for people with disabilities, and programs for the elderly. States are not required to match federal dollars and have wide discretion over how they spend the money.

categories.title: Other Family Services
categories.desc: Child welfare, family support, and protective services programs
categories.details: This covers smaller HHS programs including child abuse prevention grants, home visiting programs for new parents, services for runaway and homeless youth, and support for children in foster care. These programs connect vulnerable families with caseworkers, counselors, and community services.

This part of the budget covers early childhood, child care, and family-service programs administered primarily by HHS. Head Start and the Child Care and Development Fund together account for most of this spending and serve millions of low-income children and working families.

categories.title: Training & Employment
categories.desc: Job training and workforce programs for youth, adults, and displaced workers

categories.title: WIOA Formula Grants
categories.desc: Workforce Innovation and Opportunity Act formula grants for adult, youth, and dislocated worker training
categories.details: WIOA sends formula grants to states, which distribute money to local American Job Centers where workers can get career counseling, job search help, and paid training for in-demand occupations. PY2025 funds are split across adults facing barriers to employment ($883M), displaced workers (~$1B), and at-risk youth ($948M). States and localities have flexibility in designing local programs within federal guidelines.

categories.title: Job Corps
categories.desc: Residential education and job training program for low-income young adults ages 16–24
categories.details: Job Corps is a free, residential program that gives low-income young adults a place to live while they earn their GED, learn a trade, and get help finding a job. Students can train in fields like construction, health care, transportation, and IT at roughly 120 campuses nationwide, serving about 50,000 students per year. Most of the budget covers campus operations, staff, and student support.

categories.title: Apprenticeship & Competitive Grants
categories.desc: Registered apprenticeship, reentry employment, and other competitive workforce grants
categories.details: This funding expands Registered Apprenticeship — earn-while-you-learn programs where workers get paid on-the-job training plus classroom instruction leading to an industry-recognized credential. Grants also support reentry employment programs helping formerly incarcerated people find jobs and competitive grants to community colleges and employers building training pipelines.

categories.title: Adult Education & Employment Services
categories.desc: Adult basic education, English literacy, and Wagner-Peyser Employment Service grants
categories.details: Adult Education grants (AEFLA) help out-of-school adults get basic literacy skills, earn a high school equivalency diploma, or learn English. Wagner-Peyser Employment Service grants fund state labor exchange programs — the job boards and placement services operated through American Job Centers. FY2025 funding held at FY2024 enacted levels under the continuing resolution.

This part of the budget covers job training and workforce programs for youth and adults, administered by the U.S. Department of Labor. WIOA formula grants and Job Corps together make up the bulk of spending. The FY2025 continuing resolution held most DOL workforce programs at FY2024 levels.

categories.title: Student Financial Assistance
categories.desc: Net federal cost of Pell Grants, campus-based aid, and student loan programs after loan reestimates

categories.title: Pell Grants
categories.desc: Need-based Pell Grants for low- and middle-income college students
categories.details: Pell Grants are the largest federal college grant program — money students do not have to repay. More than 6 million students receive Pell Grants each year, and the maximum award for 2024-25 was $7,395. Funding comes from both annual discretionary appropriations (~$22.5B) and a mandatory funding stream, totaling roughly $27-29B in disbursements. Pell is the bedrock of federal college access policy and reaches students at nearly any accredited college or trade school.

categories.title: Campus-Based Aid
categories.desc: Federal Work-Study and Federal Supplemental Educational Opportunity Grants
categories.details: Campus-based programs give colleges a pool of federal funds to distribute to their neediest students. Federal Work-Study ($1.0B) subsidizes part-time jobs — often on-campus or at nonprofits — so students can earn money for expenses without borrowing. The Federal Supplemental Educational Opportunity Grant ($910M) provides additional non-repayable aid to students with exceptional financial need, with individual colleges deciding which students receive awards.

categories.title: Student Loan Net Outlays
categories.desc: Net cost or savings to the federal government from the student loan program after annual reestimates
categories.details: The federal government originates over $80 billion in student loans each year, but under federal credit accounting rules, the estimated lifetime cost (or savings) of those loans is recorded upfront. In FY2025, H.R. 1 reversed Biden-era loan forgiveness cost estimates, producing a large negative outlay — meaning the government's books recorded a net cost savings on the existing loan portfolio. This accounting swing of roughly -$233 billion at the Department level is the main reason Function 500 totals are dramatically lower in FY2025 than in FY2024. The negative number here does not mean students received less aid; it reflects a change in the estimated future cost of loans already outstanding.

The net Student Financial Assistance figure is negative in FY2025 entirely because of student loan accounting, not because grant aid fell. H.R. 1 modified income-driven repayment rules, reversing large upward cost estimates recorded in FY2024. Pell Grant disbursements and campus-based aid continued at normal levels; the negative net here offsets those real outlays within the function total.

categories.title: Other Education
categories.desc: Higher education support, education research, libraries, vocational rehabilitation, and career & technical education

categories.title: Vocational Rehabilitation
categories.desc: State grants helping people with disabilities prepare for and find employment
categories.details: Vocational Rehabilitation (VR) is a mandatory grant program that funds state agencies helping people with physical or mental disabilities return to work or find employment. Services include job counseling, skills training, assistive technology, and supported employment. States must match about 21 cents for every federal dollar, and about 600,000 people are served each year.

categories.title: Higher Education Support
categories.desc: Grants to minority-serving institutions, TRIO, GEAR UP, and other postsecondary programs
categories.details: These funds support Historically Black Colleges and Universities (HBCUs), Hispanic-Serving Institutions (HSIs), Tribal Colleges, and other minority-serving institutions that educate a disproportionate share of low-income and first-generation college students. Also included are TRIO programs that help low-income students navigate college access and completion, and GEAR UP grants that build college-going culture in middle and high schools.

categories.title: Research & Evaluation
categories.desc: Education research, national student performance data, and program evaluation through IES
categories.details: The Institute of Education Sciences (IES) funds research on what teaching methods work, collects national data on student performance (including the 'Nation's Report Card'), and evaluates whether federal education programs deliver results. This evidence base helps states, districts, and Congress make informed decisions. IES also operates regional educational laboratories that provide research assistance to local schools.

categories.title: Libraries & Museums
categories.desc: Institute of Museum and Library Services grants supporting public libraries and museums
categories.details: The Institute of Museum and Library Services (IMLS) distributes grants to public libraries, school libraries, and museums in all 50 states. Grants support children's literacy programs, digital access for rural communities, and preservation of local history. IMLS is one of the primary federal supporters of public library systems, which serve as free community learning and technology hubs.

categories.title: Career & Technical Education and Other
categories.desc: Perkins CTE grants, federally supported special institutions, and other education programs
categories.details: Perkins Career and Technical Education (CTE) grants fund high school and community college programs in fields like health care, IT, manufacturing, and agriculture — preparing millions of students for jobs that do not require a four-year degree. This category also covers federally supported institutions like Gallaudet University (serving the deaf and hard of hearing), the National Technical Institute for the Deaf, and the American Printing House for the Blind.

This part of the budget covers vocational rehabilitation, higher education support, education research, libraries, and career and technical education. Vocational Rehabilitation is the largest single program here, funded as a mandatory entitlement. The remainder covers discretionary programs that support postsecondary access, research quality, and public information infrastructure.

This part of the budget supports schools, students, workers, and family services — including K-12 grants, early childhood programs, job training, college aid, and support for people with disabilities. The FY2025 total of $71.0 billion is dramatically lower than FY2024's $304.6 billion, almost entirely because of a $233 billion swing in student loan accounting: H.R. 1 reversed Biden-era loan forgiveness cost estimates, producing large negative outlays that offset real grant and program spending. Source: U.S. Treasury FY2025 Combined Statement of Receipts, Outlays, and Balances.

categories.title: Agriculture
categories.desc: Farm safety-net payments, rural development, agricultural research, Forest Service, and USDA regulatory programs.

categories.title: Farm Income Stabilization
categories.desc: Farm safety-net programs that cushion crop losses and commodity price swings
categories.details: Federal programs that protect farmers from catastrophic losses due to weather, disease, and price swings. Includes crop insurance premium subsidies, direct commodity support payments, and working-lands conservation cost-share.

categories.title: Crop Insurance
categories.desc: Premium support and indemnities through federal crop insurance
categories.details: The federal government subsidizes a large share of farmers' crop insurance premiums so they can afford policies that pay out when crops are destroyed by drought, floods, pests, or price crashes. Private insurers sell and service the policies; USDA's Risk Management Agency backs and regulates them. The program covers over 130 commodities and roughly 284 million acres annually. FY2024 actual outlays were approximately $15 billion, with FY2025 in a similar range.

categories.title: Commodity Programs
categories.desc: Commodity support and farm safety-net programs
categories.details: Direct payments to producers of major crops — wheat, corn, soybeans, peanuts, rice, dairy, and sugar — when market prices fall below statutory reference levels or farm revenues drop below historical guarantees. The two main programs are Price Loss Coverage (PLC) and Agricultural Risk Coverage (ARC), both run by USDA's Farm Service Agency. CBO projects roughly $7–8B per year for FY2025–2034 under baseline law, though actual payments vary with prices.

categories.title: Conservation Support
categories.desc: Working-lands conservation and related farm support
categories.details: Financial and technical assistance that pays farmers and landowners to adopt practices protecting soil, water quality, and wildlife habitat on working farmland. The largest programs are the Environmental Quality Incentives Program (EQIP) and the Conservation Stewardship Program (CSP). The Conservation Reserve Program (CRP) pays farmers to take environmentally sensitive land out of production entirely, at roughly $2B per year. USDA spent approximately $5.7B on major conservation programs in a recent fiscal year.

categories.title: Rural Development
categories.desc: Housing, utility, broadband, and business support for rural communities
categories.details: Loans, loan guarantees, and grants for housing, utilities, broadband, and businesses in rural communities — areas that private capital often bypasses. Most of the volume here is in loans; the budget outlay is the net subsidy cost (the portion the government does not expect to recover), which is much smaller than total lending. USDA Rural Development primarily serves communities of fewer than 50,000 people.

categories.title: Rural Housing
categories.desc: Single-family, multifamily, and rental housing programs
categories.details: Below-market direct loans and rental assistance so low- and moderate-income rural residents can buy, build, or stay in safe housing. USDA's Section 502 direct loan program offers subsidized interest rates to buyers who can't qualify for conventional mortgages. The Rental Assistance Program covers the gap between what low-income tenants can afford and their actual rent in USDA-financed properties.

categories.title: Rural Utilities
categories.desc: Water, broadband, and electric infrastructure support
categories.details: Loans and grants to build or upgrade water systems, electric grids, and broadband internet in rural areas. The ReConnect program funds high-speed internet to communities where private providers won't build. Electric loan programs help rural cooperatives finance generation and transmission infrastructure. Budget outlays reflect the subsidy cost on loans, not total lending volume.

categories.title: Rural Business
categories.desc: Rural business and cooperative development
categories.details: Loan guarantees, grants, and technical assistance for rural small businesses, cooperatives, and entrepreneurs. The Business & Industry (B&I) loan guarantee program encourages private lenders to extend credit to rural businesses they would otherwise consider too risky. Net budget outlays are the estimated loss on guarantees, not the full loan volume.

categories.title: Agricultural Research
categories.desc: USDA science, extension, and research grants that support food and farm systems
categories.details: Federal investment in the science behind American food and farming — from disease-resistant seeds to food safety detection to climate adaptation. USDA's research mission is split between in-house laboratories (ARS) and competitive grants to universities and extension services (NIFA). The FY2025 budget requested approximately $3.8B for USDA research, education, and outreach, continuing near FY2024 enacted levels under the year-long continuing resolution.

categories.title: Agricultural Research Service (ARS)
categories.desc: Agricultural Research Service research programs
categories.details: USDA's in-house research agency, operating over 90 laboratories across the country. ARS scientists work on crop disease resistance, food contamination, pollinator health, and drought-tolerant varieties — work that benefits farmers and consumers but isn't profitable enough for private industry alone. The FY2025 budget requested $1.784B for ARS, slightly below the FY2024 enacted level of $1.845B.

categories.title: NIFA & Extension
categories.desc: Research grants, extension, and higher-education support
categories.details: Competitive and formula grants to land-grant universities, Historically Black Colleges and Universities, and cooperative extension services to conduct research and deliver findings directly to farmers and rural communities. The flagship Agriculture and Food Research Initiative (AFRI) is the nation's largest competitive grants program for agricultural science. NIFA was funded at approximately $1.68B in FY2024, with FY2025 continuing at a similar level.

categories.title: Other USDA Science
categories.desc: Other agricultural science and data programs
categories.details: Funding for the National Agricultural Statistics Service (NASS), the Economic Research Service (ERS), and other data programs that produce the official crop reports, farm income estimates, and food price data that markets and policymakers rely on. These agencies do not conduct field research but provide the statistical backbone for U.S. agricultural policy.

categories.title: Forest Service & Other USDA Programs
categories.desc: Forest Service, food safety, animal and plant health, agricultural marketing, and trade programs
categories.details: Covers the U.S. Forest Service (managing 193 million acres of national forests and grasslands, including wildland firefighting), food safety inspection (FSIS), animal and plant health programs (APHIS), agricultural marketing, foreign agricultural trade promotion (FAS), and departmental administration. The Forest Service accounts for roughly $7–8B of this total; the remainder covers regulatory and trade functions.

categories.title: International Affairs
categories.desc: Funding for U.S. diplomacy, foreign aid, and security support abroad. Covers embassy operations, humanitarian relief, global health, economic development, and military assistance to allies. This is Budget Function 150 and represents less than 1% of total federal spending.
categories.details: International Affairs (Budget Function 150) covers all U.S. government spending on diplomacy, foreign aid, and security assistance abroad. It is entirely discretionary spending — Congress votes on it every year. FY2025 was unusual: the Trump administration froze and cancelled large amounts of previously appropriated foreign assistance early in the year, and Congress separately rescinded ~$7.67 billion in July 2025 (P.L. 119-28). As a result, actual outlays of $45.2 billion were $11.2 billion (20%) below FY2024 levels. Sub-category totals are estimates based on enacted appropriations adjusted for known rescissions; account-level outlay data is available at USAspending.gov and ForeignAssistance.gov.

notableExpenses.title: PEPFAR
notableExpenses.desc: The U.S. government's flagship global HIV/AIDS effort, including treatment, prevention, and support for health systems abroad
notableExpenses.details: This uses the broader FY2025 HIV/AIDS package commonly associated with PEPFAR: $4.395 billion in State HIV/AIDS funding, $330 million in USAID HIV/AIDS funding, and a $1.65 billion U.S. contribution to the Global Fund. In plain terms, it funds anti-retroviral treatment, testing, prevention, and the international partnerships that help keep millions of people alive.

notableExpenses.title: Food for Peace
notableExpenses.desc: Emergency food aid that buys and ships U.S. commodities to people facing hunger, conflict, and displacement overseas
notableExpenses.details: Food for Peace Title II is one of the best-known U.S. humanitarian aid programs. The FY2025 enacted amount supports grants that buy food, move it through ports and shipping networks, and distribute it in crisis zones through humanitarian partners.

categories.title: Foreign Economic Aid
categories.desc: Humanitarian relief, development, and global health assistance overseas
categories.details: U.S. economic and humanitarian assistance provided overseas through USAID, the State Department, and other agencies. Includes emergency disaster relief, global health programs, and long-term development finance. FY2025 outlays were significantly lower than appropriated levels due to a funding pause and $7.67 billion in mid-year rescissions enacted by Congress (P.L. 119-28). Actual outlay totals reflect only funds that were disbursed before cancellation.

categories.title: Humanitarian Assistance
categories.desc: Refugee, food, and emergency humanitarian aid
categories.details: Emergency relief for refugees, displaced people, and disaster-affected populations overseas, funded through USAID's International Disaster Assistance (IDA) and State Department's Migration and Refugee Assistance (MRA) accounts. Originally appropriated at ~$8.7 billion for FY2025, but administration actions and rescissions reduced actual disbursements to an estimated $5.9 billion. Funds support food, shelter, medical care, and protection for people caught in conflict or natural disasters.

categories.title: Global Health
categories.desc: Global health security and disease-response assistance
categories.details: Funds U.S. global health programs including PEPFAR (HIV/AIDS), the President's Malaria Initiative, tuberculosis treatment, maternal and child health, and global health security. The Global Health Programs account was appropriated at $9.53 billion for FY2025 after a $500 million rescission, but actual outlays reached approximately $6.5 billion due to a funding pause and award cancellations early in the fiscal year. The U.S. is historically the world's largest funder of global health aid.

categories.title: Development Assistance
categories.desc: Development finance and bilateral assistance
categories.details: Bilateral economic assistance for long-term development goals such as food security, clean energy, good governance, and poverty reduction, administered through USAID's Development Assistance (DA) and Economic Support Fund (ESF) accounts. The Trump administration rescinded $2.5 billion in DA funds and $1.65 billion in ESF funds during FY2025. Actual outlays for these accounts were roughly 25–40% below FY2024 levels.

categories.title: MCC, Food Aid & Other
categories.desc: Millennium Challenge Corporation, international food aid, and other economic assistance
categories.details: Includes the Millennium Challenge Corporation (MCC), which funds compact agreements with lower-income countries to reduce poverty through economic growth; international food aid programs (Food for Peace Title II); and assessed contributions to international organizations such as the UN and WHO. The FY2026 budget proposed a 75.9% cut to MCC. Combined CIO and CIPA (UN dues and peacekeeping assessments) were funded at $2.45 billion in FY2025.

categories.title: Diplomacy
categories.desc: Embassies, diplomatic operations, and consular support run by the State Department
categories.details: State Department operations including U.S. embassies and consulates worldwide, the Foreign Service workforce, diplomatic security, and consular services. Title I of the annual SFOPS bill funds these activities. Total enacted funding for State Department and USAID operations was approximately $12.2 billion for FY2025, though some funds were subject to rescission.

categories.title: Diplomatic Programs
categories.desc: Embassy operations, diplomacy, and foreign service support
categories.details: Covers the day-to-day cost of running U.S. diplomacy: salaries and benefits for Foreign Service and Civil Service employees, embassy operations, policy work, and international negotiations. This is the core operating budget of the State Department. It also funds public diplomacy and cultural exchange programs like the Fulbright scholarship.

categories.title: Embassy Security & Construction
categories.desc: Embassy security, construction, and maintenance
categories.details: Pays for physical security upgrades, new embassy construction, and maintenance of U.S. diplomatic facilities worldwide through the Embassy Security, Construction, and Maintenance (ESCM) account. Funding has grown following high-profile security incidents at overseas posts. The FY2025 enacted level was approximately $3.5 billion.

categories.title: Consular & International Organizations
categories.desc: U.S. consular operations and contributions to international organizations
categories.details: Covers U.S. consular operations (passport and visa processing, American citizen services abroad) and assessed U.S. contributions to over 40 international organizations including the United Nations regular budget, NATO, WHO, and IAEA. Combined UN dues and peacekeeping contributions (CIO + CIPA) were funded at approximately $2.45 billion for FY2025.

categories.title: Foreign Military Aid
categories.desc: Security assistance that helps allied and partner nations purchase U.S. defense equipment, build their military capacity, and participate in peacekeeping. Administered primarily through the State Department's Title IV security assistance accounts. Israel ($3.3B), Egypt ($1.3B), and Jordan are consistently among the largest recipients of Foreign Military Financing.

notableExpenses.title: Ukraine Military Aid
notableExpenses.desc: U.S. security assistance to Ukraine, including Foreign Military Financing and other support
notableExpenses.details: FMF grants enable purchase of U.S. defense articles/services. Supports Ukraine's self-defense and regional partners affected by conflict, funding equipment, training, and capacity building to deter aggression.

notableExpenses.title: Israel Military Aid
notableExpenses.desc: U.S. security assistance that helps Israel purchase American defense equipment through Foreign Military Financing
notableExpenses.details: This is the recurring FY2025 base Foreign Military Financing amount for Israel, separate from the large supplemental enacted in April 2024. In practice, FMF works like a grant that Israel uses to buy U.S.-made defense equipment and services.

categories.title: Foreign Military Financing (FMF)
categories.desc: Grants for allies to purchase U.S. defense equipment
categories.details: Grants that allow foreign governments to purchase U.S.-made defense equipment and services. Israel receives $3.8 billion annually under a 10-year memorandum of understanding; Egypt receives $1.3 billion per year. FMF is the largest single security assistance account. In practice, most FMF funds are spent on U.S. defense contracts, supporting American defense industry jobs.

categories.title: Peacekeeping & Security Programs
categories.desc: Peacekeeping and broader security-assistance programs
categories.details: Funds U.S. contributions to UN peacekeeping missions and broader security assistance programs including the Economic Support and Stabilization Fund, Nonproliferation and Antiterrorism programs (NADR), and the Global Security Contingency Fund. These programs help partner nations maintain stability, counter terrorism, and clear land mines.

categories.title: Military Training & Other Aid
categories.desc: IMET and other military training support
categories.details: Covers the International Military Education and Training (IMET) program, which brings foreign military officers to the U.S. for professional training, and other smaller capacity-building programs. IMET funding is typically under $200 million per year. Also includes counter-narcotics assistance and programs to support foreign police and justice sector reforms.

categories.title: Science, Space & Technology
categories.desc: Civilian research and space programs across NASA, NSF, and other agencies

categories.title: NASA
categories.desc: America's civilian space agency, covering science missions, astronaut exploration, aviation research, and mission support
categories.details: NASA explores space, studies Earth and the universe, develops new aerospace technology, and runs major human spaceflight programs. This budget includes everything from telescopes and planetary probes to Moon missions, flight operations, engineering support, and aeronautics research. The figures here follow NASA's FY2025 budget request.

notableExpenses.title: Artemis Campaign
notableExpenses.desc: NASA's Moon program, including the systems needed to send astronauts back to lunar orbit and the lunar surface
notableExpenses.details: Artemis is NASA's effort to return astronauts to the Moon and build the systems needed for longer-term exploration. NASA's FY2025 budget request highlighted about $7.8 billion for Artemis-related work. This is a mission-level highlight that spans multiple NASA accounts rather than a separate add-on to the total.

notableExpenses.title: International Space Station
notableExpenses.desc: Funding to keep the ISS operating while NASA prepares for commercial stations in low Earth orbit
notableExpenses.details: NASA's FY2025 budget request included about $1.2696 billion for the International Space Station. That supports station operations, research on board, and planning for the shift to commercial replacements later in the decade. In simple terms, it keeps the station running while NASA prepares for what comes next.

categories.title: Deep Space Exploration Systems
categories.desc: Moon to Mars hardware and other systems for human exploration beyond low Earth orbit
categories.details: This category funds the hardware and systems NASA uses for deep-space human exploration. It includes the Moon to Mars effort, such as transportation systems, lunar systems development, and exploration architecture. In simple terms, this is the core budget for building and preparing the next big human missions.

categories.title: Space Operations
categories.desc: International Space Station, launch support, communications networks, and other day-to-day flight operations
categories.details: This pays for operating missions that are already flying or supporting launches. It covers the International Space Station, space communications, launch services, and other mission operations. Think of it as the part of NASA that keeps space activities running every day.

categories.title: Space Technology
categories.desc: New technologies that could make future missions safer, cheaper, or more capable
categories.details: This budget develops and tests tools NASA may need for future missions. That includes things like advanced propulsion, in-space manufacturing, robotics, and other technologies before they are ready for routine use. It is basically NASA's future-toolbox budget.

categories.title: Science
categories.desc: Earth science, planetary science, astrophysics, heliophysics, and space biology research
categories.details: This supports NASA's robotic science missions and research programs. It includes studying Earth, the Sun, planets, the universe, and how living systems behave in space. These are many of the missions people think of when they picture NASA discoveries.

categories.title: Aeronautics
categories.desc: Research to improve aircraft safety, efficiency, and lower-emission flight
categories.details: NASA does aviation research as well as space work. This money supports safer and quieter aircraft, better air traffic systems, and technologies that can cut fuel use and emissions. It is the part of NASA focused on flight inside Earth's atmosphere.

categories.title: STEM Engagement
categories.desc: Education and outreach programs that connect students and educators to NASA's work
categories.details: This supports NASA education and public engagement efforts. It helps students, teachers, and communities connect with NASA missions through learning programs and outreach. It is small compared with mission budgets, but it helps build the next generation of scientists and engineers.

categories.title: Safety, Security & Mission Services
categories.desc: The workforce, IT, facilities support, and mission assurance needed to run NASA safely
categories.details: This is the support structure that keeps NASA functioning. It pays for mission assurance, engineering support, cybersecurity, buildings, workforce costs, and other agency-wide services. In plain terms, it is the operating backbone behind NASA's missions.

categories.title: Construction & Environmental Compliance
categories.desc: Construction, modernization, and environmental cleanup work at NASA facilities
categories.details: This funds upgrades and repairs to NASA buildings, launch facilities, and other infrastructure. It also covers environmental compliance and restoration work at agency sites. These dollars help keep NASA's physical footprint usable and safe.

categories.title: Inspector General
categories.desc: Independent oversight and audits of NASA programs and operations
categories.details: This pays for independent watchdog work inside NASA. The Inspector General audits programs, investigates misconduct, and looks for waste, fraud, or abuse. It helps Congress and the public see whether NASA is managing money responsibly.

categories.title: National Science Foundation
categories.desc: The main federal agency for basic research grants, research facilities, and STEM education
categories.details: NSF mostly sends money out as grants to universities, nonprofits, and research teams rather than running large missions itself. It funds basic research, research infrastructure, and education and workforce programs across science and engineering. The figures here follow NSF's FY2025 budget request.

categories.title: Research & Related Activities
categories.desc: NSF's main research account for grants, institutes, computing, polar science, and research partnerships
categories.details: This is NSF's biggest account by far. It funds the core research portfolio across fields like biology, computing, engineering, math, social science, and polar research. In simple terms, this is where most NSF grant money lives.

categories.title: STEM Education
categories.desc: Programs that support STEM learning, teacher development, scholarships, and workforce training
categories.details: This supports STEM learning from early education through graduate school and into the workforce. It includes scholarships, teacher programs, undergraduate and graduate training, and efforts to broaden participation in STEM. The goal is to help more people build science and engineering skills.

categories.title: Major Research Facilities
categories.desc: Construction and acquisition of large scientific facilities and major mid-scale infrastructure
categories.details: This account pays for very large research tools and facilities that are too big for ordinary grant programs. Examples include major computing systems, observatories, and other shared infrastructure used by many researchers. It is the part of NSF that helps build the scientific tools themselves.

categories.title: Agency Operations & Award Management
categories.desc: Staff, systems, and administration needed to review, award, and oversee NSF funding
categories.details: This pays for the people and systems that run NSF. It covers proposal review, grant oversight, IT, staffing, and other behind-the-scenes operations. It is how NSF manages a very large grant portfolio with a relatively small staff.

categories.title: Oversight & Board
categories.desc: The Inspector General and National Science Board
categories.details: This small slice covers independent oversight and NSF's governing board. The Inspector General handles audits and investigations, while the National Science Board helps guide policy and strategy. Together they make up a very small share of NSF's budget.

categories.title: DOE Office of Science
categories.desc: Basic research and national-lab science in physics, computing, fusion, energy, and large user facilities
categories.details: DOE's Office of Science is the federal government's biggest funder of basic research in the physical sciences. It supports university researchers, national labs, and large shared facilities used by scientists across the country. The figures here follow the FY2025 Office of Science budget request.

categories.title: Advanced Scientific Computing Research
categories.desc: Supercomputing, advanced software, networking, and computing research
categories.details: This funds DOE's high-end computing and computational science work. It supports supercomputers, networking, advanced software, and research that helps scientists solve problems too large for ordinary computers. In short, it powers the computing side of frontier science.

categories.title: Basic Energy Sciences
categories.desc: Materials science, chemistry, and major user facilities such as light sources and neutron sources
categories.details: This is DOE's largest Office of Science program. It supports basic research in materials, chemistry, and related fields, along with major scientific facilities that thousands of researchers use. A lot of foundational energy and materials science starts here.

categories.title: Biological & Environmental Research
categories.desc: Climate, environmental systems, bioenergy, genomics, and related biological research
categories.details: This supports research on biological systems, environmental processes, climate, and related tools and facilities.
