It helps scientists understand how living systems and the environment work, including energy-relevant biology and Earth systems. It is one of DOE's main bridges between physical science and life and climate science.

categories.title: Fusion Energy Sciences
categories.desc: Fusion research, plasma science, and U.S. contributions to major fusion facilities
categories.details: This funds the basic science and experimental work behind fusion energy. It supports plasma physics, fusion materials research, and U.S. participation in major facilities like ITER. The goal is to build the scientific base needed for future fusion power.

categories.title: High Energy Physics
categories.desc: Particle physics, accelerators, neutrino experiments, and related facilities
categories.details: This funds research on the smallest building blocks of matter and the forces that shape the universe. It includes particle accelerators, detector projects, and large experiments such as neutrino research. It is the part of DOE focused on answering very fundamental physics questions.

categories.title: Nuclear Physics
categories.desc: Research on atomic nuclei, nuclear matter, and the facilities used to study them
categories.details: This program studies the atomic nucleus and how nuclear matter behaves. It supports large facilities, experiments, and the development of new tools like the Electron-Ion Collider. In plain English, it focuses on the structure and behavior of matter at the nuclear level.

categories.title: Other Office of Science Programs
categories.desc: Isotopes, lab infrastructure, management, and other smaller Office of Science activities
categories.details: This combined bucket covers the smaller parts of the Office of Science budget that still matter a lot. It includes isotope production, science laboratory infrastructure, and program direction and management. These funds help keep the research system running and support work that does not fit the larger science accounts.

categories.title: NOAA Research & Satellites
categories.desc: NOAA science, research infrastructure, and weather and space-weather satellite programs
categories.details: This groups the NOAA pieces most people think of as science and space work. It combines NOAA research and development with major weather and space-weather satellite funding. Because NOAA also handles fisheries, oceans, and forecasting operations, this is a science-focused slice rather than the agency's full budget.

categories.title: Research & Development
categories.desc: Research, development, and research facilities across NOAA's science offices
categories.details: This covers NOAA research and development spending, including equipment and facilities tied to that work. It supports climate, oceans, atmosphere, environmental data, modeling, and research tools across multiple NOAA offices. In simple terms, it is NOAA's core science and research budget.

categories.title: Weather Satellites
categories.desc: Current and next-generation weather and space-weather satellite systems
categories.details: This funds NOAA's weather satellite systems and the next generation of satellites being built to replace them. These spacecraft feed the forecasts and environmental data people rely on every day. It is one of the biggest and most visible technology investments inside NOAA.

categories.title: U.S. Geological Survey
categories.desc: Earth science, hazard monitoring, mapping, ecosystems, water, and resource research
categories.details: USGS provides science about hazards, maps, water, ecosystems, and natural resources. It is the agency behind a lot of the earthquake sensors, streamgages, geologic mapping, and Landsat-related science the public depends on. The figures here follow the FY2025 USGS budget request.

categories.title: Ecosystems
categories.desc: Science on wildlife, invasive species, environmental health, climate effects, and land management
categories.details: This supports research on ecosystems, species, environmental health, and how land and climate change affect them. It helps managers make decisions about conservation, invasive species, disease, and habitat. It is the biological and ecological side of USGS.

categories.title: Energy & Mineral Resources
categories.desc: Science on critical minerals, energy resources, and the environmental effects of extracting them
categories.details: This category studies where energy and mineral resources are, how much exists, and what the environmental tradeoffs are. It includes critical minerals, geothermal assessments, and other resource analyses. The work helps inform energy and supply-chain decisions.

categories.title: Natural Hazards
categories.desc: Earthquakes, volcanoes, landslides, coastal hazards, geomagnetism, and related warning science
categories.details: This pays for science and monitoring tied to hazards that can threaten lives and property. It includes earthquake sensors, volcano observatories, landslide work, coastal hazards, and geomagnetism. These are some of USGS's most directly public-facing safety programs.

categories.title: Water Resources
categories.desc: Streamgages, groundwater, water quality, water availability, and related monitoring and research
categories.details: This supports the water data and science used by communities, farmers, utilities, and emergency managers. It includes streamgages, groundwater monitoring, water quality work, and studies of drought and water availability. In plain terms, it helps track where water is, what condition it is in, and how it is changing.

categories.title: Core Science Systems
categories.desc: Maps, geospatial data, Landsat-related science, and foundational Earth data systems
categories.details: This is the part of USGS that provides the foundational mapping and data layers other science depends on. It includes geospatial systems, imagery, data platforms, and work tied to Landsat and other shared information systems. It is the backbone data infrastructure for a lot of USGS science.

categories.title: Mission Support & Other
categories.desc: Administration, facilities, and other USGS activities outside the main mission areas
categories.details: This combined bucket covers the rest of the USGS request outside the major science mission areas. It includes support functions, administration, and other agency-wide work needed to operate labs, field programs, and data systems. It is the support layer that keeps the mission areas working.

categories.title: National Institute of Standards and Technology
categories.desc: Measurement science, standards, manufacturing programs, and research facilities
categories.details: NIST develops the measurements, standards, and technical tools that support science, industry, and technology policy. It also funds manufacturing competitiveness programs and upgrades to research facilities. The figures here follow NIST's FY2025 budget request.

categories.title: Scientific & Technical Research and Services
categories.desc: NIST's core research labs, measurement science, and standards work
categories.details: This is NIST's main research account. It supports the labs and experts who develop measurement science, test methods, standards, and technical guidance across fields like AI, cybersecurity, quantum, manufacturing, and physics. In simple terms, it funds the science NIST is best known for.

categories.title: Construction of Research Facilities
categories.desc: Construction, modernization, and repair of NIST research buildings and infrastructure
categories.details: This pays for repairing and modernizing NIST's research facilities. It covers building upgrades, maintenance backlogs, utilities, and specialized infrastructure needed for advanced scientific work. These funds help make sure the labs are safe and capable enough for modern research.

categories.title: Industrial Technology Services
categories.desc: Manufacturing Extension Partnership and Manufacturing USA
categories.details: This supports NIST programs that help American manufacturers adopt better tools and processes. It includes the Hollings Manufacturing Extension Partnership and Manufacturing USA. The aim is to strengthen industrial competitiveness and spread new manufacturing technology more quickly.

This section is organized by agency instead of by OMB budget function, so the total will not add up to the overall Science budget due to overlapping responsibilities and funding streams. It groups together the FY2025 budget requests for major civilian science and space agencies and science-focused agency components that people are most likely to recognize.

categories.title: General Government
categories.desc: Core operations of the federal government itself: Congress, the White House, Treasury, and the agencies that keep the government running day to day.
categories.details: This is the part of the budget that keeps the federal government itself running. It covers tax collection, managing federal debt, running Congress, operating the White House, and managing federal buildings and employees. This is entirely discretionary — Congress votes on it every year. The total here is from the FY2025 Combined Statement of Receipts, Outlays, and Balances (U.S. Treasury), which reports $34.5 billion net. The OMB Historical Table 3.2 reports $40.5 billion gross before deducting $2.5 billion in offsetting receipts.

categories.title: Central Fiscal Operations
categories.desc: IRS tax collection, the Bureau of the Fiscal Service (debt payments and accounting), and Treasury Departmental Offices including sanctions enforcement.
categories.details: This is OMB budget subfunction 803. It is the largest slice of general government spending and includes everything needed to collect taxes and manage the government's money. The IRS alone received about $12.3 billion in FY2025 appropriations to process returns, enforce tax law, and answer taxpayer questions — that work funds virtually all other government programs. The Bureau of the Fiscal Service runs payment systems that send out Social Security checks, tax refunds, and other federal payments to hundreds of millions of Americans each year.

categories.title: IRS
categories.desc: Tax administration, enforcement, and taxpayer services.
categories.details: The IRS collected roughly $5 trillion in taxes in FY2025 — every dollar the government spends on Social Security, defense, and health care flows through collections the IRS manages. This funding pays for customer service phone lines, online filing systems, audit and enforcement staff, and IT modernization. Without this investment, the government would collect far less than it spends, widening the deficit. The IRS appropriation for FY2025 was held flat at $12.3 billion for the third consecutive year.

categories.title: Bureau of the Fiscal Service
categories.desc: Federal payment systems, debt financing, and government-wide accounting.
categories.details: The Bureau of the Fiscal Service is the government's payment processor and accountant. It issues Social Security checks, tax refunds, and federal payroll — handling over one billion payments a year. It also manages the borrowing that finances the federal deficit by auctioning Treasury securities. This office publishes USAspending.gov and Fiscal Data so the public can see how their money is spent.

categories.title: Treasury Departmental Offices
categories.desc: Policy leadership, sanctions enforcement, and financial intelligence including OFAC and FinCEN.
categories.details: This covers the parts of Treasury that set economic policy, enforce financial sanctions, and fight money laundering. The Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) administers sanctions programs that freeze the assets of adversaries like Russia, Iran, and North Korea. The Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (FinCEN) tracks suspicious financial transactions to combat drug trafficking and terrorism financing. The amount here also includes the Community Development Financial Institutions (CDFI) Fund, which directs capital to underserved communities.

categories.title: Legislative Branch
categories.desc: Congressional operations including the House, Senate, GAO, CBO, Library of Congress, and Capitol Police.
categories.details: This is OMB budget subfunction 801. It funds the 535 members of Congress, their staffs, and the agencies that support legislative work. The Government Accountability Office (GAO) uses this funding to audit how executive agencies spend money and publishes hundreds of oversight reports each year. The Congressional Budget Office (CBO) provides independent cost estimates for every major bill before it passes. The Library of Congress — the world's largest library — is also funded here, serving both Congress and the public.

categories.title: Congressional Operations
categories.desc: House, Senate, and joint legislative operations including member offices, committees, and leadership.
categories.details: This pays for every senator's and representative's office, committee staff, floor operations, and legislative support services. Each member of Congress has a staff that handles constituent services — helping Americans navigate problems with Social Security, VA benefits, immigration cases, and federal agencies. The roughly 10,000 congressional staff who work in these offices are often the first point of contact between ordinary Americans and the federal government.

categories.title: Library of Congress
categories.desc: Library of Congress collections, copyright office, and research services.
categories.details: The Library of Congress holds over 175 million items — books, maps, photographs, recordings, and manuscripts — making it the largest library in the world. It provides research services directly to Congress and runs the U.S. Copyright Office, which registers intellectual property protections for millions of American creators each year. The library also offers free public access to its digital collections online.

categories.title: GAO
categories.desc: Government Accountability Office — the independent auditor of the federal government.
categories.details: The GAO is Congress's watchdog, auditing how federal agencies use taxpayer money. It publishes hundreds of reports each year identifying waste, fraud, and inefficiency across government. GAO recommendations have historically saved billions of dollars — in FY2024 alone, implemented GAO recommendations saved an estimated $56.7 billion. Every dollar spent on GAO has generated roughly $114 in documented savings over recent years.

categories.title: Capitol Complex & Other
categories.desc: Capitol Police, Architect of the Capitol, CBO, and other legislative support agencies.
categories.details: This covers the agencies that keep the Capitol campus functioning and provide independent analysis. The Capitol Police protect members of Congress, staff, and visitors. The Architect of the Capitol maintains the Capitol building, the Supreme Court building, and the Library of Congress facilities. The Congressional Budget Office scores every major piece of legislation with an independent cost estimate before a vote.

categories.title: General Purpose Fiscal Assistance
categories.desc: Payments to Washington D.C., U.S. territories (Puerto Rico, Guam, etc.), and tax-sharing payments.
categories.details: This is OMB budget subfunction 806. The largest piece is a direct federal payment to Washington D.C., which cannot raise the same tax revenue as a state but must fund city services like police, schools, and courts. Payments to U.S. territories including Puerto Rico, Guam, the U.S. Virgin Islands, and the Northern Mariana Islands are also included here, compensating for the fact that these jurisdictions have limited fiscal authority and their residents pay federal taxes but receive less federal program funding than states.

categories.title: Other General Government
categories.desc: GSA (federal buildings and procurement), OPM (federal workforce management), and other government-wide management.
categories.details: This combines OMB subfunctions 804 (General property and records management), 805 (Central personnel management), and 808 (Other general government). The General Services Administration (GSA) manages over 370 million square feet of federal office space and runs centralized procurement that saves money through bulk purchasing. The Office of Personnel Management (OPM) administers retirement and health benefits for roughly 2.7 million active federal employees and millions of retirees. The net figure for property management is nearly zero in FY2025 because offsetting receipts from building rentals offset most gross spending.

categories.title: Executive Direction and Management
categories.desc: White House and Executive Office units including OMB, NSC, and other presidential management offices.
categories.details: This is OMB budget subfunction 802. It covers the operational costs of the Executive Office of the President — the White House staff, the Office of Management and Budget (OMB), the National Security Council, and related policy offices. OMB coordinates the entire federal budget process, reviewing every agency's spending plan before the President's budget is submitted to Congress. This relatively small figure reflects direct operating costs only; the programs these offices oversee are funded in other budget functions.

categories.title: Energy
categories.desc: Civilian energy programs at the Department of Energy, including clean energy research, efficiency programs, grid resilience, and the Strategic Petroleum Reserve.
categories.details: This covers non-nuclear-weapons spending by the Department of Energy and related agencies. FY2025 outlays were $20.9 billion — a 51.5% increase over FY2024's $13.8 billion, largely driven by Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) funding for clean energy deployment and a major restocking of the Strategic Petroleum Reserve. This function does not include DOE's nuclear weapons activities, which fall under National Defense.

notableExpenses.title: Diablo Canyon Power Plant Support (CA)
notableExpenses.desc: Federal support to keep California's Diablo Canyon nuclear plant operating instead of shutting down on its original retirement schedule.
notableExpenses.details: DOE says the maximum four-year Civil Nuclear Credit award value for Diablo Canyon is up to $1,100,520,444 and is subject to annual adjustment. To keep this receipt on a FY2025 basis, this notable expense uses an annualized estimate of $275.1 million by splitting the four-year cap evenly across the four-year award period. This is an inference for year-by-year display, not a DOE-published FY2025 payment figure.

categories.title: Energy Supply
categories.desc: DOE programs for clean energy deployment, nuclear power, fossil energy research, and grid resilience.
categories.details: This is OMB budget subfunction 271. It is the largest energy subfunction and funds research, demonstration projects, and deployment support across all major power technologies. Clean energy deployment programs received a large boost from Inflation Reduction Act funding, supporting projects like utility-scale battery storage, advanced geothermal, and next-generation nuclear reactors. Grid resilience investments help protect the electrical grid against extreme weather and cyberattacks.

categories.title: Clean Energy Deployment
categories.desc: Loan guarantees, grants, and demonstration projects for solar, wind, battery storage, and other clean technologies.
categories.details: This funds DOE's Loan Programs Office and technology demonstration projects that help bring new clean energy technologies from the lab to commercial scale. Loan guarantees lower the financial risk for projects like advanced nuclear plants, offshore wind, and long-duration energy storage that are too novel for private lenders alone. These programs aim to create manufacturing jobs and reduce electricity costs over time.

categories.title: Nuclear Energy
categories.desc: Research and development for advanced nuclear reactors and fuel-cycle programs.
categories.details: Nuclear power currently provides about 18% of U.S. electricity and nearly half of all carbon-free electricity. This funding supports research into advanced reactor designs — including small modular reactors (SMRs) and microreactors — that could be cheaper and faster to build than traditional large plants. It also funds work on fuel recycling and waste management to reduce the long-term burden of nuclear waste storage.

categories.title: Fossil & Carbon Management
categories.desc: Research on carbon capture, hydrogen, and cleaner use of fossil fuels.
categories.details: This funds DOE's National Energy Technology Laboratory (NETL) and programs focused on capturing carbon dioxide from power plants and industrial facilities before it enters the atmosphere. It also supports hydrogen production research, which could provide clean fuel for industries like steel and shipping that are hard to electrify. These programs are aimed at reducing emissions while natural gas and coal remain part of the energy mix.

categories.title: Grid & Other Supply
categories.desc: Grid modernization, transmission infrastructure, and other energy supply programs.
categories.details: A reliable electrical grid is the backbone of the U.S. economy. This funding goes toward upgrading transmission lines to carry more renewable power, improving cybersecurity for grid control systems, and building energy storage that can balance supply and demand as more intermittent wind and solar comes online. The Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) and the electricity marketing administrations (such as the Bonneville Power Administration) are also funded here.

categories.title: Energy Conservation
categories.desc: Weatherization grants, state efficiency programs, federal building upgrades, and efficiency research.
categories.details: This is OMB budget subfunction 272. These programs help households, businesses, and government reduce the amount of energy they use — which lowers energy bills and reduces pollution. The Weatherization Assistance Program helps low-income households insulate their homes, upgrade heating and cooling systems, and reduce drafts, cutting their energy bills by an average of hundreds of dollars a year. Federal building efficiency upgrades save taxpayer money on utility bills in the long run.

categories.title: Weatherization & State Programs
categories.desc: Grants to states for home weatherization and energy efficiency, primarily for low-income households.
categories.details: The Weatherization Assistance Program provides funding to states and localities to upgrade the homes of income-qualifying households — adding insulation, sealing air leaks, and replacing inefficient furnaces and water heaters. These upgrades typically cut household energy bills by $300–$500 a year on a permanent basis, helping low-income families afford other necessities. The State Energy Program (SEP) provides flexible grants to states to develop their own energy efficiency and renewable energy strategies.

categories.title: Federal Energy Management
categories.desc: Efficiency upgrades for federal buildings, vehicles, and operations.
categories.details: The federal government is the single largest energy consumer in the U.S., occupying over 300,000 buildings and operating a fleet of more than 600,000 vehicles. This funding pays for efficiency upgrades — LED lighting, better HVAC systems, rooftop solar, and electric vehicles — that reduce long-term utility and fuel costs for taxpayers. Congress requires federal agencies to meet energy intensity reduction targets each year.

categories.title: Efficiency R&D
categories.desc: Research on building, industrial, and vehicle efficiency technologies.
categories.details: This funds DOE's Building Technologies, Industrial Efficiency, and Vehicle Technologies offices. Research here has produced innovations like LED lighting (which now saves Americans roughly $14 billion a year compared to incandescent bulbs), highly efficient heat pumps, and advanced materials for electric vehicle batteries. The goal is to push down the cost of efficient technologies so the private market adopts them at scale.

categories.title: Other Conservation
categories.desc: Energy efficiency standards, consumer information programs, and other conservation efforts.
categories.details: This includes the appliance and equipment efficiency standards program, which sets minimum efficiency levels for refrigerators, air conditioners, water heaters, and other products. These standards are estimated to save households hundreds of dollars over the life of an appliance and reduce the need for new power plants. The ENERGY STAR program (run jointly with EPA) helps consumers and businesses identify efficient products.

categories.title: Strategic Petroleum Reserve & Emergency Preparedness
categories.desc: The Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR), Northeast Home Heating Oil Reserve, and related emergency fuel stockpiles.
categories.details: This is OMB budget subfunction 274. The Strategic Petroleum Reserve is the world's largest emergency oil stockpile — up to 714 million barrels stored in underground salt caverns in Louisiana and Texas. The government released roughly 180 million barrels from the SPR in 2022 to combat gasoline price spikes after Russia's invasion of Ukraine; this funding covers the ongoing effort to replenish those stocks, maintain the storage sites, and operate the Northeast Home Heating Oil Reserve, which protects consumers in the Northeast from winter supply disruptions.

categories.title: Strategic Petroleum Reserve
categories.desc: Core SPR oil purchases, storage operations, and site maintenance.
categories.details: The SPR holds crude oil that can be released into the market within days when supply disruptions threaten the economy — such as after a hurricane, a pipeline failure, or a geopolitical crisis. The U.S. can release up to 4.4 million barrels per day from the reserve. After large releases in 2022, this funding supports buying oil back to restore reserve levels, as well as maintaining the aging infrastructure of the four storage sites.

categories.title: Northeast Reserves
categories.desc: Regional heating oil and gasoline reserves for the Northeast U.S.
categories.details: The Northeast Home Heating Oil Reserve holds about 1 million barrels of heating oil to protect homeowners in New England — one of the few U.S. regions that still relies heavily on heating oil — from supply disruptions during winter. A separate Northeast Gasoline Supply Reserve was also maintained here. These relatively small stockpiles are designed to provide bridge supply while the broader market adjusts to a disruption, not to replace the full SPR.

categories.title: Site Operations & Cleanup
categories.desc: Environmental remediation and ongoing maintenance of SPR storage facilities.
categories.details: The four SPR sites — Bryan Mound, Big Hill, West Hackberry, and Bayou Choctaw — require ongoing maintenance to keep salt caverns structurally sound and equipment operational. Some older infrastructure requires environmental remediation to address leaks or contamination from decades of oil storage. This funding ensures the reserve can respond quickly in an emergency.

categories.title: Energy Information, Policy, and Regulation
categories.desc: Energy Information Administration (EIA) data collection and analysis, and other energy regulatory functions.
categories.details: This is OMB budget subfunction 276. The Energy Information Administration (EIA) is the primary source of independent energy data for the United States — tracking oil and gas prices, electricity generation, energy consumption by sector, and long-term energy outlooks. Its weekly petroleum status reports move global oil markets. This small but influential office also includes funding for energy regulatory functions and policy analysis offices that inform major energy decisions.
